summary: DHH celebrates five antitrust bills introduced in the US House of Representatives targeting big tech monopoly abuses. He reviews each bill with increasing enthusiasm, from sensible measures like merger filing fees and data portability to bolder proposals banning acquisitions by dominant platforms and prohibiting self-preferencing. The American Choice and Innovation Online Act is his favorite, as it would end forced in-app purchasing, default app bundling, and developer gag orders. He sees Lina Khan's FTC confirmation and bipartisan support as reasons for optimism, and notes parallel efforts in Europe.
key_points: Five House antitrust bills range from modest (merger fees, data portability) to revolutionary (acquisition bans, platform separation, ending self-preferencing)
The American Choice and Innovation Online Act would ban forced in-app purchasing, default app bundling, developer gag orders, and platform self-preferencing
Lina Khan's confirmation as FTC chair provides an ideal enforcer who helped shape the modern antitrust framework these bills embody
Bipartisan sponsorship of every bill makes passage a realistic possibility in a deeply divided Congress
Global antitrust momentum is building simultaneously, with Germany's Bundeskartellamt pursuing Apple under new competition law
money_quotes: For over a decade, the likes of Google, Apple, Amazon, and Facebook have been able to get away with murder in digital markets without fear of consequences.
You're telling me that Facebook wouldn't have been able to buy Instagram and WhatsApp, as a matter of basic law, not a prolonged merger approval inquiry? That's bold.
The bill prevents tying the use of other services, like payment processing, to the ability to publish on the platform.
This essentially turns the app stores into common carriers.
There's some poetic justice in those rates, as they're exactly the same as the payment extortion fees demanded by Apple and Google of some app developers.
She's big tech's worst nightmare and the dream chair for the rest of us.
Opposition to big tech's monopoly abuses is one of the very few truly bipartisan issues in Washington these days.
Big tech is about to meet the one real obstacle to world domination: Sovereign governments with democratic mandates.
key_insight: After years of unchecked monopoly power, a combination of bold bipartisan legislation, a determined FTC chair, and global regulatory momentum finally presents a credible threat to big tech's dominance.
